title: Matching Character Sets
concept: Using wildcards, ranges, and character classes (like \d for digits, \w for words) to build flexible search filters.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use character sets in search patterns?

options.text: To search for long sentences only
To match any single character from a specific group
To delete letters from a document

exercises.explain: A character set allows you to match any single character out of a specific group, making your searches flexible.

cards.title: The Search Shortcut
cards.body: Imagine you are searching a massive list of usernames, and you need to find all the ones that contain a number. Writing a search for '1', then '2', then '3' would take forever. What if you could use a magic shortcut to find any number instantly? That's the power of character sets!

cards.title: What are Character Sets?
cards.body: Character sets and shortcuts are secret codes in pattern matching. Instead of searching for just one specific letter, they let you search for categories. For example, \d looks for any digit (0-9), \w looks for any letter or number, and brackets like [a-z] look for any lowercase letter in that range.

cards.title: The VIP Guest List
cards.body: Think of character sets like a VIP guest list at a club. Instead of writing down every single name (like checking for 'a', then 'b', then 'c'), you just write a rule: 'Anyone with a golden ticket gets in!' A character set like [a-z] is that rule, instantly checking if a character belongs to the allowed group.

cards.title: Vowel Finder Example
cards.body: If you search for the pattern 'c[aeiou]t', it will match 'cat', 'cot', and 'cut', because the middle character must be one of the vowels inside the brackets. It will not match 'cart' because it looks for exactly one character from the set.

cards.title: The Bracket Trap
cards.body: Many people think that putting letters inside brackets like [cat] will search for the whole word 'cat'. In reality, it only searches for a single character that is either a 'c', an 'a', or a 't'! Brackets match exactly one character from the list.

cards.title: Quick Comparison
cards.body: See how character sets zoom in on exactly what you want to find by grouping options together.

right.text: Matches any single letter or digit (like a or 5)

exercises.prompt: Which of these characters will match the pattern [a-z]?
exercises.explain: The pattern [a-z] only matches lowercase letters. The string 'H' is uppercase, and '3' is a number, so they do not match. 'h' is a lowercase letter, which matches perfectly.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The pattern [a-z] matches any lowercase letter from 'a' to 'z'.
exercises.explain: The hyphen inside brackets creates a range. [a-z] matches any lowercase letter from a to z, saving you from typing out all 26 letters.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to find any single digit in a text.
exercises.sentence: To match any single number, we use the shortcut ____.
exercises.explain: The shortcut \d specifically searches for any single numerical digit from 0 to 9.

exercises.prompt: Three coding assistants explain the difference between \d and \w. Who is correct?

options.text: AI-1: '\d matches any word character, and \w only matches numbers.'
AI-2: '\d matches any digit (0-9), while \w matches letters and numbers.'
AI-3: 'There is no difference; both \d and \w match the exact same characters.'

exercises.explain: Only AI-2 correctly identifies that \w matches both letters and numbers, while \d is restricted to numerical digits (0-9). AI-1 gets them reversed, and AI-3 wrongly states they do the same thing.

exercises.prompt: You want to find a code that starts with one uppercase letter, followed by exactly one number, and ends with one lowercase letter. Which of these codes matches the pattern [A-Z]\d[a-z]?
exercises.explain: The set [A-Z] matches any single uppercase letter, \d matches any single digit, and [a-z] matches any single lowercase letter. Combined as [A-Z]\d[a-z], this pattern exactly matches 'B9g'. '9Bg' starts with a digit, 'Bg9' ends with a digit, and 'B99' ends with a digit instead of a lowercase letter.
